5: nephele photeinen — a bright/luminous cloud — the cloud of divine presence (shekinah) throughout the OT (Exod 13:21, 40:34-38, 1 Kgs 8:10-11); the Transfiguration cloud is the visible form of the divine glory-presence investing itself in the scene. akouete autou — listen to him — the addition to the baptism-declaration (3:17) echoes Deut 18:15 (him you shall listen to = the prophet like Moses); the Transfiguration completes the Moses/Elijah pattern by declaring that the one who surpasses them is the one to be heard.
1: kai meth hēmeras hex paralambanei ho Iēsous ton Petron kai Iakōbon kai Iōannēn ton adelphon autou — after six days Jesus takes Peter and James and John his brother (meth hēmeras hex: after six days — the specific time-reference connects this to the confession of 16:16; only the three members of the inner group are taken; cf. the same three at Gethsemane in 26:37) — kai anapherei autous eis oros hypsēlon kat idian (and leads them up on a high mountain privately — anapherei: to lead/carry up, an ascent-verb used in Heb 13:15 for offering sacrifice): the mountain is the locus of divine encounter throughout Matthew.
2: kai metemorphōthē emprosthen autōn — and he was transfigured before them (metemorphōthē aorist passive of metamorphoō: to transform in form — not a change of outer costume but a radiance emerging from within; the passive voice implies the action is done to Jesus or occurs in him) — kai elampsen to prosōpon autou hōs ho hēlios (and his face shone like the sun — elampsen aorist of lampō: to shine, to radiate): the solar comparison echoes Moses's face after Sinai (Exod 34:29-35) but exceeds it: Moses's face reflected divine glory; Jesus's face is the source of its own radiance. Ta de himatia autou egeneto leuka hōs to phōs — his garments became white as the light.
3: kai idou ōphthē autois Mōysēs kai Ēlias synlalounte met autou — and behold, there appeared to them Moses and Elijah talking with him (ōphthē aorist passive of horaō: were seen, appeared — the passive of revelation; Mōysēs kai Ēlias: Moses and Elijah represent the Law and the Prophets; Luke 9:31 adds that they spoke of Jesus's "departure/exodus" — exodon): the two figures who represent the entire Hebrew Scripture appear alongside Jesus, placing him as the fulfillment and culmination of both streams. Synlalounte met autou — conversing with him: an active dialogue, not a mere appearance.
4: apokritheis de ho Petros eipen tō Iēsou kyrie kalon estin hēmas hōde einai — Peter answering said to Jesus: Lord, it is good for us to be here (apokritheis: answering — used even where no prior question was asked; Peter responds to the situation) — ei theleis poiēsō hōde treis skēnas (if you wish I will make here three tents/booths — skēnas: tents, booths, tabernacles; the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot involved building booths): Peter's impulse to build three equal shelters for Jesus, Moses, and Elijah misunderstands the hierarchy — Moses and Elijah are witnesses to Jesus, not equals. The cloud-voice corrects the equivalence.
6: kai akousantes hoi mathētai epesan epi prosōpon autōn kai ephobēthēsan sphodra — hearing this the disciples fell on their faces and were very afraid (epesan epi prosōpon autōn: they fell on their faces — the prostration response to theophany; cf. Ezek 1:28; Dan 8:17; Rev 1:17; ephobēthēsan sphodra: were greatly afraid — sphodra: intensely, exceedingly): the divine voice from the cloud produces prostration, not the light or the figures. The voice is the locus of maximum terror, because it directly addresses them as hearers.
7: kai prosēlthen ho Iēsous kai hapsamenpos autōn eipen — and Jesus coming touched them and said (hapsamenps aorist participle of haptō: to touch — the healing/comforting touch; Jesus has not departed; he is physically present and responds to their prostration with a touch): egerthēte kai mē phobeisthe (rise up and do not be afraid — egerthēte aorist passive imperative of egeirō: the resurrection verb used for ordinary rising; mē phobeisthe present imperative: do not be in a state of fear). The first word after the theophany is "rise up" — the resurrection vocabulary applied to the disciples' prostration.
8: eparantes de tous ophthalmous autōn oudena eidon ei mē auton Iēsoun monon — lifting up their eyes they saw no one except Jesus alone (eparantes tous ophthalmous: lifting up the eyes — the gesture of recovery from prostration, looking up after the fall; monon: alone, only): Moses and Elijah have withdrawn; the cloud has lifted. What remains after the theophany is Jesus alone. The singularity of "Jesus alone" is the interpretive key to the Transfiguration: Law and Prophets give way; Jesus stands as their fulfillment and summation.
9: kai katabainontōn autōn ek tou orous eneteilato autois ho Iēsous legōn — as they were coming down from the mountain Jesus commanded them saying (eneteilato aorist of entellomai: to command, give an injunction) — mēdeni eipēte to horama heōs hou ho huios tou anthrōpou ek nekrōn egerthē (tell the vision to no one until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead — to horama: the vision, a visionary experience — the word for prophetic seeing; heōs hou...egerthē: until the Son of Man is raised — the resurrection is the condition for disclosure: the Transfiguration makes sense only from the other side of the resurrection).
10: kai epērōtēsan auton hoi mathētai legontes — the disciples asked him saying — ti oun hoi grammateis legousin hoti Ēlian dei elthein prōton (why then do the scribes say Elijah must come first? — Ēlian dei elthein prōton: Elijah must come first; the expectation is Mal 4:5): the disciples' question shows they understood the Transfiguration's Elijah-appearance as potentially the fulfillment of the Elijah-first expectation. Jesus's answer distinguishes between Elijah-in-type (John the Baptist, who has already come) and Elijah-as-person (who may still have an end-time role).
11: ho de apokritheis eipen Ēlias men erchetai kai apokatastēsei panta — answering he said: Elijah indeed comes and will restore all things (apokatastēsei future of apokathistēmi: to restore to an original or proper state — the eschatological restoration word; cf. Acts 1:6; the same root as apokatastasis) — Jesus affirms the Elijah-first tradition as real; the question is whether it has been fulfilled or is still future. The present tense erchetai (comes, is coming) followed by the future apokatastēsei (will restore) suggests an ongoing or future role, not a completed one.
12: legō de hymin hoti Ēlias ēdē ēlthen — but I tell you that Elijah has already come (ēdē ēlthen: has already come — the aorist with the adverb of current relevance) — kai ouk epegnōsan auton alla epoiēsan en autō hosa ēthelēsan (and they did not recognize him but did to him whatever they wished — ouk epegnōsan: did not recognize — the intensive recognition-verb in the negative; John came in the Elijah-role but was unrecognized): houtōs kai ho huios tou anthrōpou mellei paschein hyp autōn (so also the Son of Man is about to suffer at their hands): John's fate previews Jesus's fate; the pattern of rejection-of-God's-messenger repeats.
13: tote synēkan hoi mathētai hoti peri Iōannou tou baptistou eipen autois — then the disciples understood that he spoke to them about John the Baptist (synēkan aorist of syniēmi: the understanding-breakthrough; the aorist marks the moment of comprehension): the understanding that Elijah-who-came = John-the-Baptist becomes explicit; they had the pieces from 11:14 (where Jesus said "if you are willing to accept it, he is Elijah who is to come") but the Transfiguration context makes it clear. The disciples' understanding is developing through the Gospel by a series of such breakthrough moments.
14: kai elthontōn pros ton ochlon prosēlthen autō anthrōpos gonyp etōn kai — coming to the crowd, a man came to him kneeling (gonyp etōn: kneeling, genuflecting — the posture of petition before authority; the same form of approach as the Canaanite woman in 15:25) — the scene shifts from the mountain of transfiguration to the ordinary crowd with its suffering and the disciples' inability to help. The contrast between the mountain-glory and the valley-failure intensifies both events: the disciples' spiritual position has not translated into healing capacity.
15: kyrie eleēson mou ton huion hoti selēniazetai kai kakōs paschei — Lord, have mercy on my son for he is a lunatic and suffers terribly (selēniazetai: to be "moon-struck" — from selēnē: moon; the term for a condition associated with the lunar cycle, epilepsy or seizure disorder: kakōs paschei: suffers terribly — the generic suffering-formula) — pollakis gar piptei eis to pyr kai pollakis eis to hydōr (for often he falls into fire and often into water): the symptoms describe violent, dangerous falls associated with demonic seizure; the medical description and the demonic explanation coexist in the ancient framework.
16: kai prosēnenka auton tois mathētais sou kai ouk ēdunēthēsan auton therapeusai — I brought him to your disciples and they were unable to heal him (ouk ēdunēthēsan aorist passive of dynamai: were not able — a total inability, not partial success): the failure is stark. The disciples were given authority over unclean spirits in 10:1 and 10:8; their failure here will be explained by Jesus in terms of faith-size rather than authority-absence. The father's report to Jesus, after the disciples' failure, is an implicit appeal that what the disciples lacked, Jesus possesses.
17: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen ō genea apistos kai diestrammenē — answering Jesus said: O unbelieving and perverted generation (apistos: without faith, faithless — the opposite of pistos/faithful; diestrammenē perfect passive participle of diastrephō: to twist thoroughly, to pervert — a generation that has been twisted out of its proper orientation) — heōs pote meth hymōn esomai heōs pote anexomai hymōn (how long will I be with you, how long will I put up with you — anexomai: to bear with, endure, put up with — a patience-exhaustion expression): the rebuke is addressed to the generation, not narrowly to the disciples, though the disciples' failure is the trigger.
18: kai epetimēsen autō ho Iēsous kai exēlthen ap autou to daimonion — Jesus rebuked it and the demon came out of him (epetimēsen aorist of epitimaō: to rebuke, command — the standard exorcism-rebuke verb; exēlthen aorist: went out completely): kai etherapeuthē ho pais apo tēs hōras ekeinēs (and the boy was healed from that hour — tēs hōras ekeinēs: from that hour — the same formula as 15:28 and 9:22, marking instantaneous healing): the cure is immediate; the preceding diagnosis and the coming explanation of the disciples' failure frame it as a teaching event about faith.
19: tote proselthontes hoi mathētai tō Iēsou kat idian eipon — then the disciples came to Jesus privately and said (kat idian: privately — the private teaching context that follows difficult public events throughout Matthew) — dia ti hēmeis ouk ēdynēthēmen ekbalein auto (why were we unable to drive it out? — ekbalein: to drive out — the exorcism verb): the question is sincere; the disciples do not doubt Jesus's authority but cannot explain their own failure. Jesus's answer addresses the root cause: faith-size, not authority-level.
20: ho de legei autois dia tēn oligopistian hymōn — he says to them: because of your little faith (oligopistian: little faith — the noun form of the compound oligopistos; the only use of the noun in Matthew; the accusation is general, describing the disciples' faith-level as insufficient) — amen gar legō hymin ean echēte pistin hōs kokkos sinapeōs (for truly I tell you, if you have faith like a mustard seed — kokkos sinapeōs: a grain of mustard, the proverbially smallest seed; here the comparison is not to the tree but to the grain): ereite tō orei toutō metabēthi enteuthen ekei kai metabēsetai — you will say to this mountain: move from here to there, and it will move. Kai ouden adynatēsei hymin — nothing will be impossible for you.
21: touto de to genos ouk ekporeuetai ei mē en proseuchē kai nēsteia — but this kind does not go out except by prayer and fasting — the verse is absent from some early manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) but present in others and in Mark 9:29 (without "fasting"). If original to Matthew, it specifies that certain severe demonic cases require an intensified prayer-and-fasting posture beyond the general authority granted in 10:1. The note qualifies the faith-as-mustard-seed saying of v.20 without contradicting it: mustard-seed faith that operates through prayer and fasting.
22: systrephomenōn de autōn en tē Galilaia eipen autois ho Iēsous — as they were gathering in Galilee Jesus said to them — mellei ho huios tou anthrōpou paradidosthai eis cheiras anthrōpōn (the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men — paradidosthai present passive infinitive of paradidōmi: to be delivered, handed over — the betrayal/passion vocabulary): the second passion prediction uses paradidosthai, linking the passion directly to the Judas-betrayal that will occur in ch26. The irony of "delivered into the hands of men" is that "Son of Man" (a divine figure) is handed over to human power.
23: kai apoktenousin auton kai tē tritē hēmera egerthēsetai — and they will kill him, and on the third day he will be raised (egerthēsetai future passive of egeirō: will be raised — the divine passive: God raises the Son of Man who was killed by men) — kai elypēthēsan sphodra (and they were deeply grieved — elypēthēsan sphodra: were distressed exceedingly; sphodra: intensely): the disciples heard the passion prediction and were grieved without full understanding (Luke 9:45 notes they did not grasp it). The grief is genuine; the comprehension is partial; they cannot yet hold together death and resurrection as a unity.
24: elthontōn de autōn eis Kapharnaoun prosēlthon hoi ta didrachma lambanontes tō Petrō — coming to Capernaum those who collect the two-drachma tax came to Peter (didrachma: two drachmas — the annual temple tax of half a shekel, based on Exod 30:13-16, collected for the Jerusalem Temple; every adult Jewish male paid this; the collectors approach Peter, not Jesus, perhaps because Peter is recognizable as the group's spokesperson): ho didaskalos hymōn ou telei ta didrachma (does not your teacher pay the two-drachma tax?): the question is not hostile but administrative.
25: legei nai kai elthonta eis tēn oikian proephthasen auton ho Iēsous legōn — he says: yes. And coming into the house Jesus anticipated him saying (proephthasen aorist of prophthanō: to get ahead of, to anticipate — Jesus speaks first before Peter can report the exchange) — ti soi dokei Simōn hoi basileis tēs gēs apo tinōn lambanousin telē ē kēnson (what do you think, Simon? The kings of the earth — from whom do they collect toll or tax?): the question is practical and political: apo tōn huiōn autōn ē apo tōn allotriōn (from their own sons or from strangers?).
26: eipantos de apo tōn allotriōn ephē autō ho Iēsous ara ge eleutheroi eisin hoi huioi — when he said: from the strangers, Jesus said to him: then the sons are free (ara ge: therefore indeed/then certainly — a strong logical inference; eleutheroi eisin hoi huioi: the sons are free, exempt — eleutheros: free person, not slave; free from the obligation): the argument: earthly kings exempt their own children from taxation; if God is the Father and the Temple is his house, then the Son of God is exempt from the Temple tax. The analogy is compressed but clear.
27: hina de mē skandalisōmen autous — but so that we do not cause them to stumble (skandalisōmen aorist subjunctive of skandalizō: the stumbling-cause verb; mē + subjunctive: a prohibition of purpose) — the tax is voluntarily paid not because it is owed but to avoid causing offense. Poreutheis eis thalassan bale agkistron (going to the sea, throw out a hook — agkistron: a fishhook, not a net; a single targeted catch): kai ton prōton ichthyn anabanta aron (and taking the first fish that comes up — ton prōton: the very first): kai anoixas to stoma autou heurēseis statēra — opening its mouth you will find a stater (a four-drachma coin = one shekel, covering the tax for two people).
